---
title: "PROCSI C Training"
subtitle: "Two-Week Summer Camp, Summer 2026"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-13
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "PROCSI C Training  |  UW-Madison"
    embed-resources: true
---

## How the camp works {.smaller}

\

- Each **morning**, a short brief: today's topic, your target, and where to start. Then good luck.
\
- The lab tours in the College of Engineering are scattered throughout the week, so we will do them on different days and at different times. But the times will be known at the beginning of the camp.
\
- You work in **teams**, hands-on, all day (i.e., whenever you do not have lunch or lab tours). **Claude is your always-on tutor.**

---

## How the camp works: afternoons & more {.smaller}

- Late afternoon (**3:30 to 4:30**), each team gives a friendly **~10-minute** talk: how far you got and what you learned.
    - Begin every talk with your team's **quote of the day**.
    - Length of the talk: 10 minutes. Strictly enforced.
    - Use the projector in the meeting room along with your laptop.

/

- **No grades, no quizzes.** Discover new things, make new friends, and enjoy a little friendly competition.
- All our meetings and work sessions will be in room 1153ME in the Mechanical Engineering building at UW-Madison.
    - No food or drinks are allowed in the room.


## Teams

- We pick **5 captains**. Captains take turns (round-robin) drafting teammates into **5 teams**.
    - No captain can be a captain again during one week; they can be a captain again the next week.
- **New teams each day**, so you keep meeting new people.
    - Captains take turns drafting teammates from the pool of people who have not been drafted yet.
- Teams compete good-naturedly; at day's end, everyone **votes for a favorite** summary of the day.
- The only stakes are bragging rights and fun.

## Before you start

- Bring along your own **laptop** (at least one good one per team).
- Each team member needs a free **Onshape** account: it runs in the browser, nothing to install.
- We install **Miniforge (conda)** and **PyChrono** together, no prior setup needed.
- Curiosity required; experience not.

# Week 1

## Week 1 at a glance

| Day | Focus |
|-----|-------|
| Mon, Jul 13 | Welcome, College of Engineering, meet everyone |
| Tue, Jul 14 | Command line interface (CLI) and bash; meet Claude |
| Wed, Jul 15 | Python (conda) and a little git |
| Thu, Jul 16 | Blender: render your first image |
| Fri, Jul 17 | Onshape mechanism, then render it in Blender |

## Monday, July 13

- CSULA cohort arriving late
- [AM, 1153ME]{.am} Overview of the College of Engineering at UW-Madison
- [PM, 1153ME]{.pm} How the camp works; getting to know each other
- [PM]{.pm} Ice cream expedition at Memorial Union; walk there after dinner

## Tuesday, July 14

- [AM]{.am} The command line interface (CLI) and bash: files, folders, navigation; `git clone` as just another command
- [AM]{.am} Meet Claude, your tutor for the whole camp
- [PM]{.pm} Hands-on in teams; ~10-minute talks and the first quote of the day

## Wednesday, July 15

- [AM]{.am} Install Miniforge (conda); Python basics: variables, loops, functions, arrays
- [AM]{.am} Just-enough git: `add`, `commit`, `push` to save your work. What is GitHub, how does it work, and what is the interplay between GitHub and your local machine, and between GitHub and git?
- [PM]{.pm} Python exercises with Claude; team talks

## Thursday, July 16

- [AM]{.am} Blender basics; render a still image
- [PM]{.pm} Teams render a scene; team talks
- [PM]{.tour} Lab tour

## Friday, July 17

- [AM]{.am} Onshape: build a simple mechanism (slider-crank or four-bar); export
- [PM]{.pm} Import the CAD part into Blender, set a scene, render; team talks

# Week 2

## Week 2 at a glance

| Day | Focus |
|-----|-------|
| Mon, Jul 20 | What does "simulate" mean? The sliding brick and forward Euler numerical integration |
| Tue, Jul 21 | Turn the math into a Blender movie of the brick |
| Wed, Jul 22 | PyChrono: the same brick for real, plus wow demos |
| Thu, Jul 23 | AI-assisted modeling with RAG (chrono-rag) |
| Fri, Jul 24 | State Street send-off; pack up |

## Monday, July 20

- [AM]{.am} The sliding brick: mass m, force f, over 10 s; the equation of motion; forward Euler
- [PM]{.pm} Teams code the Euler loop in Python and plot position vs time; team talks
- [PM]{.tour} Lab tour

## Tuesday, July 21

- [AM]{.am} From numbers to motion: turn the Euler output (a CSV) into a Blender keyframe animation
- [PM]{.pm} Teams render their brick movie over a real backdrop; team talks

## Wednesday, July 22

- [AM]{.am} Install PyChrono (conda); the **same brick**, now with real contact and friction
- [AM]{.am} Two demos: a slider-crank mechanism, and a rover on terrain
- [PM]{.tour} Lab tour
- [PM]{.pm} Teams run and tweak a demo; team talks

## Thursday, July 23

- [AM]{.am} What is RAG? Use chrono-rag  to generate a PyChrono model, then run it
- [PM]{.pm} Bigger models, generated with chrono-rag, or picked up from existing repositories; team talks

## Friday, July 24

- [AM]{.am} Head to State Street and purchase swag; help the Madison economy.
- [PM]{.pm} Pack up; get ready for Saturday departure
